checklist: 'can'은 현재 또는 일반적인 능력을 나타낼 때 사용한다.
'could'는 과거의 능력 또는 현재에 대한 공손한 요청/제안을 나타낼 때 사용한다.
정중하게 요청하거나 제안할 때는 'could you...?'를 사용한다.
wrong_example: I can swim when I was a child.
correct_example: I could swim when I was a child.
original_description:
# **Action verbs** describe physical or mental action.
#***Transitive verbs** take a direct and/or indirect object.
#***Intransitive verbs** take no object.
(Note: Passive verbs always are intransitive, but they become transitive when changed to the active mood.)
#**Linking verbs** connect the subject of a sentence to its complements: a predicate noun, a predicate pronoun or a predicate adjective. They do not express action and thus may be considered intransitive.  They include:
#*The forms of “to be,” (*am*, *is*, *are*, *was*, *were*, *be*, *being*, *been*, *become* and *became*.)
#*Various verbs related to the senses (*appear*, *feel*, *look*, *smell*, *sound*, *taste*, etc.)
#*Verbs expressing condition or placement of the subject (*become*, *grow*, *remain*, *seem*, *stay*, etc.)
#**Auxiliary verbs** are used to inflect various verb tenses, moods, etc.  The auxiliary verbs are:
#*The forms of “to be,” (*am*, *is*, *are*, *was*, *were*, *be*, *being*, *been*.)
#*The forms of “ha...

checklist: 'must'는 강한 의무나 확신을 나타낸다.
'have to'는 의무를 나타내지만 'must'보다 더 일반적인 상황에서 쓰인다.
'have to'는 부정문에서 'don't/doesn't/didn't have to'로 의무의 부재를 나타낸다.
wrong_example: I must to go to the store.
correct_example: I must go to the store.

checklist: 'would'는 과거의 습관을 나타내거나 공손한 요청을 할 때 사용된다.
가정법에서 'if + 과거형 동사'와 함께 사용하여 현재 혹은 미래의 상상 상황을 서술한다.
wrong_example: If I knew, I will tell you.
correct_example: If I knew, I would tell you.
original_description:
# **Action verbs** describe physical or mental action.
#***Transitive verbs** take a direct and/or indirect object.
#***Intransitive verbs** take no object.
(Note: Passive verbs always are intransitive, but they become transitive when changed to the active mood.)

checklist: 'wish' 다음에는 현실과 반대되는 현재 상황을 가정할 때 과거형 동사를 사용한다.
'be동사'는 모든 주어에 대해 'were'로 변환한다.
wrong_example: I wish I am taller.
correct_example: I wish I were taller.
original_description:
## Moods of Verbs
Mood identifies the manner in which a verb expresses an idea.
### The Three Moods of Verbs
#The indicative mood states a fact or asks a question.
#The subjunctive mood is used to express:
#*a wish or a condition that is contrary to fact.
#*a command or request after the word that.
#The imperative mood gives a command or makes a request. Verbs in this mood are always in the present tense and second person.
Note: The indicative and subjunctive moods have the same forms except for the following:
*In the third=person singular, the -s is omitted from verbs.
*The form of the verb to be is always be in the present subjunctive mood.
*The form of the verb to be is always were in the past subjunctive mood.

checklist: 'wish' 다음에 과거에 있었던 것을 후회하거나 아쉬움을 나타낼 때 과거완료 형태를 사용한다.
이 표현은 과거 상황과 반대되는 사실을 나타낸다.
wrong_example: I wish I didn't eat that much.
correct_example: I wish I hadn't eaten that much.
original_description:
## Moods of Verbs
Mood identifies the manner in which a verb expresses an idea.
### The Three Moods of Verbs
#The indicative mood states a fact or asks a question.
#The subjunctive mood is used to express:
#*a wish or a condition that is contrary to fact.
#*a command or request after the word that.
#The imperative mood gives a command or makes a request. Verbs in this mood are always in the present tense and second person.
Note: The indicative and subjunctive moods have the same forms except for the following:
*In the third=person singular, the -s is omitted from verbs.
*The form of the verb to be is always be in the present subjunctive mood.
*The form of the verb to be is always were in the past subjunctive mood.

checklist: 'if only'는 현재 상황에 대한 아쉬움을 나타낼 때 과거형 동사와 함께 사용한다.
'be' 동사는 모든 주어에 대해 'were'로 변환한다.
wrong_example: If only I was rich.
correct_example: If only I were rich.
original_description:
## Tenses of Verbs
A verb takes different forms to show when an action occurs. These forms are called tenses. The table below shows the six basic tenses (the three simple tenses and the three perfect tenses) and the special forms of a verb: the six progressive forms and two emphatic forms. Problems in using the tenses are also included.
Note: In the formation of the passive voice, only two progressive forms are included.
### Simple Tense
#### Simple Present Tense
The simple present tense encompasses the past, present and future time.

checklist: 'if only'는 과거의 상황에 대한 아쉬움을 나타낼 때 과거완료 동사를 사용한다.
이 표현은 실제 상황과 반대되는 과거의 사실을 나타낸다.
wrong_example: If only we didn't miss the bus.
correct_example: If only we hadn't missed the bus.
original_description:
## Tenses of Verbs
A verb takes different forms to show when an action occurs. These forms are called tenses. The table below shows the six basic tenses (the three simple tenses and the three perfect tenses) and the special forms of a verb: the six progressive forms and two emphatic forms. Problems in using the tenses are also included.
Note: In the formation of the passive voice, only two progressive forms are included.
### Simple Tense
#### Simple Present Tense
The simple present tense encompasses the past, present and future time.

checklist: 'should have'는 과거의 잘못된 판단에 대한 후회를 표현할 때 사용된다.
이 표현은 '과거에는 그렇게 하지 않았지만, 하지 못해서 후회한다'는 의미를 나타낸다.
wrong_example: You should go there earlier.
correct_example: You should have gone there earlier.

checklist: 'could have'는 과거에 가능성이 있었으나 실행되지 않은 일을 표현할 때 사용된다.
'could have'는 '그랬을 수도 있었지만 실제로는 그러지 않았다'는 의미를 담고 있다.
wrong_example: I could win if I tried harder.
correct_example: I could have won if I had tried harder.

checklist: 'might have'는 과거에 일어났을지 모르는 일에 대한 불확실한 추측을 나타낸다.
이 표현은 '그랬을 수도 있지만 확실하진 않다'는 의미를 전달한다.
wrong_example: He might be late yesterday.
correct_example: He might have been late yesterday.

checklist: to부정사가 주어 위치에 올 때 동사의 원형을 사용했는가?
문장의 주어로서 적절한 의미를 전달하고 있는가?
wrong_example: To studying is hard.
correct_example: To study is hard.

checklist: 명사를 수식하는 형용사가 현재분사 형태인가?
형용사가 수식하는 명사가 현재도 진행 중이거나 계속되는 상태인가?
wrong_example: A interest book was on the table.
correct_example: An interesting book was on the table.
original_description:
#### Types of Limiting Adjectives
***Numerical adjectives** may be cardinal (one, two, three,...) or ordinal *first, second, third,...)
***Identifying adjective**s are such, same and similar.
***Articles** are *a* and *an* (indefinite articles) and *the* (definite articles).
***Nouns used as adjectives** modify another noun or a pronoun.
***Pronoun Used as Adjectives**
****Demonstrative adjectives** are this, these, that and those.
****Possessive adjectives** show ownership. They are my, your, his, her, its, our and their.
****Interrogative adjectives** ask questions. They are which, what, whose and whose. When used they should immediately be followed by a noun.
****Indefinite adjectives** are grouped into three:
***used with singular nouns - another, each, either, little, much, neither and one.
***used with plural nouns - both, few, many and several.
***used with singular or plural nouns - all, any, more, most, other and some.
***Verbs are used with adjectives** when they modify a ...

checklist: 명사를 수식하는 형용사가 과거분사 형태인가?
수식받는 명사가 이미 완료된 상태나 영향을 받은 상태인가?
wrong_example: He is an excite boy.
correct_example: He is an excited boy.

checklist: to부정사가 목적을 나타내는 문장에서 사용하는가?
목적을 나타내는 의미가 문맥상 적절한가?

correct_example: I went to the library to study.

checklist: Check if the singular noun needs an article (a/an/the).
Ensure the noun represents one item or entity.
Verify that context allows for singular use.
wrong_example: I bought book yesterday.
correct_example: I bought a book yesterday.
original_description:
### Noun
# A word is a noun if it takes the subject or object position in a sentence.
# Countable nouns have two forms, singular and plural. The plural form of most words is marked by the plural ending (s/es/ies).

core_formula: Uncountable nouns do not take a/an
checklist: Verify that the noun is indeed uncountable in English.
Do not use 'a/an' with uncountable nouns.
Check for correct use of quantifiers like 'some', 'much', 'a lot of'.
wrong_example: I need an advice.
correct_example: I need some advice.
original_description: **Uncountable noun**: This kind of noun is used for things that can not be counted. ex: rice, water.

checklist: Determine if the noun is specific and known to both the speaker and listener.
Use with unique entities or previously mentioned nouns.
Avoid using 'the' with general or unspecified nouns.
wrong_example: The science is interesting.
correct_example: Science is interesting.
original_description:
#### Types of Limiting Adjectives
***Numerical adjectives** may be cardinal (one, two, three,...) or ordinal *first, second, third,...)
***Identifying adjective**s are such, same and similar.
***Articles** are *a* and *an* (indefinite articles) and *the* (definite articles).
***Nouns used as adjectives** modify another noun or a pronoun.
***Pronoun Used as Adjectives**
****Demonstrative adjectives** are this, these, that and those.
****Possessive adjectives** show ownership. They are my, your, his, her, its, our and their.
****Interrogative adjectives** ask questions. They are which, what, whose and whose. When used they should immediately be followed by a noun.
****Indefinite adjectives** are grouped into three:
***used with singular nouns - another, each, either, little, much, neither and one.
***used with plural nouns - both, few, many and several.
***used with singular or plural nouns - all, any, more, most, other and some.
***Verbs are used with adjectives** when they modify a ...

checklist: Use 'a' before consonant sounds and 'an' before vowel sounds.
Ensure the noun is singular and countable.
Check that the noun is not specified.
wrong_example: I saw a elephant.
correct_example: I saw an elephant.
original_description:
#### Types of Limiting Adjectives
***Numerical adjectives** may be cardinal (one, two, three,...) or ordinal *first, second, third,...)
***Identifying adjective**s are such, same and similar.
***Articles** are *a* and *an* (indefinite articles) and *the* (definite articles).
***Nouns used as adjectives** modify another noun or a pronoun.
***Pronoun Used as Adjectives**
****Demonstrative adjectives** are this, these, that and those.
****Possessive adjectives** show ownership. They are my, your, his, her, its, our and their.
****Interrogative adjectives** ask questions. They are which, what, whose and whose. When used they should immediately be followed by a noun.
****Indefinite adjectives** are grouped into three:
***used with singular nouns - another, each, either, little, much, neither and one.
***used with plural nouns - both, few, many and several.
***used with singular or plural nouns - all, any, more, most, other and some.
***Verbs are used with adjectives** when they modify a ...

core_formula: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
checklist: Confirm the pronoun matches the intended antecedent.
Ensure correct case usage (subjective, objective, possessive).
Check that plurals and singulars are used correctly.
wrong_example: Me saw her yesterday.
correct_example: I saw her yesterday.
original_description:
#### Cases of Personal Pronouns
#**Nominative case** - the pronoun is used as a subject or predicate nominative/subjective complement.
Example:
 *I* am beautiful.(subject)
The winner is *she*.(predicate nominative/subjective complement)
#**Objective case** - the pronoun is used as a direct object, indirect object or object of the preposition. 
Example:
 We met *them* in Florida. (direct object) 
 You have to award *him* the medal. (indirect object)
 Are you finally going out with *her*? (object of the preposition)
#**Possessive case** - the pronoun is used to show ownership or possession. 
Example:
That folder is *mine*.
These paintings are *theirs*.
### Compound Personal Pronouns
Compound personal pronouns are formed by adding -self or -selves to some personal pronouns.
#### Reflexive Pronoun
**Reflexive pronoun** may be used as a direct object or indirect object. It usually comes after the verb.
#### Intensive Pronoun
**Intensive pronoun** is used to emphasize that the action is d...

checklist: Use '-er' for short adjectives and 'more' for longer adjectives.
Ensure the comparison is clear and valid.
Check that 'than' follows the comparative adjective to complete the comparison.
wrong_example: She is more taller than her sister.
correct_example: She is taller than her sister.
original_description: For adjectives with more than two syllables, we always add more before the adjective to form its comparative, and most to form its superlative. For example, the two forms of 'comfortable' are 'more comfortable' and '(the) most comfortable'.

checklist: Check that the intensifier correctly modifies the adjective or adverb.
Ensure the use of intensifiers matches the context in terms of emphasis.
Avoid overuse which can lead to redundancy.
wrong_example: He is very tall more than his brother.
correct_example: He is much taller than his brother.
original_description: An adverb that modifies and add emphasis to an adjective and another adverb is called an **intensifier**. Some intensifiers are *barely, fully, hardly, little, quite, rather, really, scarcely, too* and *very*.

core_formula: [주어] + 동사 원형 (3인칭 단수일 경우 동사에 -s/-es 추가)
checklist: 주어와 동사의 수가 일치하는지 확인한다.
3인칭 단수 주어일 경우 동사에 -s/-es를 붙였는지 확인한다.
wrong_example: He go to school every day.
correct_example: He goes to school every day.
original_description: The principal parts are a verb’s basic forms, from which its other forms can be created by inflection or conjugation.  In English, there are three principal parts.  These are the simple present indicative, the simple past indicative, and the past perfect indicative.

checklist: 주어에 맞는 과거형 동사를 사용했는지 확인한다.
불규칙 동사의 과거형을 정확하게 사용했는지 확인한다.
wrong_example: She go to the park yesterday.
correct_example: She went to the park yesterday.
original_description: The principal parts are a verb’s basic forms, from which its other forms can be created by inflection or conjugation.  In English, there are three principal parts.  These are the simple present indicative, the simple past indicative, and the past perfect indicative.

checklist: 문장의 동작의 결과를 받는 주체가 주어로 사용되었는지 확인한다.
[Be 동사]의 시제가 주어 및 문맥에 맞게 사용되었는지 확인한다.
주된 동사가 p.p.로 변형되었는지 확인한다.
wrong_example: The letter writes by her.
correct_example: The letter is written by her.
original_description:
## Tenses of Verbs
A verb takes different forms to show when an action occurs. These forms are called tenses. The table below shows the six basic tenses (the three simple tenses and the three perfect tenses) and the special forms of a verb: the six progressive forms and two emphatic forms. Problems in using the tenses are also included.
Note: In the formation of the passive voice, only two progressive forms are included.
### Simple Tense
#### Simple Present Tense
The simple present tense encompasses the past, present and future time.

checklist: 단수 주어는 단수 동사를, 복수 주어는 복수 동사를 사용했는지 확인한다.
집합 명사나 불가산 명사가 주어일 경우 적절한 동사를 사용했는지 확인한다.
wrong_example: The team are winning.
correct_example: The team is winning.

checklist: 문장 내에 부사구나 부정어가 포함되어 도치가 필요한 경우인지 확인한다.
조동사 또는 Be 동사와 주어의 위치가 바뀌었는지 확인한다.
wrong_example: Never I have seen such a thing.
correct_example: Never have I seen such a thing.
original_description: This Wikibook is designed to teach standard **English Grammar** as taught in English-speaking countries. Although the [English](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/English) page has a separate section for grammar, it seems harder to follow if you are using it for self study or classroom use. If you plan on taking a course for grammar, this resource may help. The layout and structure is based upon the 1963 edition of *Warriner's English Grammar and Composition*, but does not use any resources or text derived from that resource. All text in this Wikibook is self-made or taken from another public domain resource. As this Wikibook is new, it is very easy to add any additional information about particular parts of a part of speech (i.e. helping verbs, subordinate clauses, etc...).  Any additions to this Wikibook are greatly appreciated.

checklist: 강조하고자 하는 부분이 [It is/was ... that ...] 형식을 따르고 있는지 확인한다.
강조 구문으로 인해 문장의 의미가 달라지지 않았는지 확인한다.
wrong_example: It was yesterday I saw him.
correct_example: It was yesterday that I saw him.
original_description: This Wikibook is designed to teach standard **English Grammar** as taught in English-speaking countries. Although the [English](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/English) page has a separate section for grammar, it seems harder to follow if you are using it for self study or classroom use. If you plan on taking a course for grammar, this resource may help. The layout and structure is based upon the 1963 edition of *Warriner's English Grammar and Composition*, but does not use any resources or text derived from that resource. All text in this Wikibook is self-made or taken from another public domain resource. As this Wikibook is new, it is very easy to add any additional information about particular parts of a part of speech (i.e. helping verbs, subordinate clauses, etc...).  Any additions to this Wikibook are greatly appreciated.

checklist: 주절과 종속절을 연결하는 접속사가 적절하게 사용되었는지 확인한다.
두 절 사이의 논리적인 연결성이 명확한지 확인한다.
wrong_example: She was tired she didn’t sleep well.
correct_example: She was tired because she didn’t sleep well.
